flashcards.front: Which linear structure is ideal for implementing browser back/forward navigation?
flashcards.back: Two stacks - one for back history and another for forward navigation.

flashcards.front: What makes arrays suitable for dynamic programming table storage?
flashcards.back: O(1) random access enables efficient lookup of computed subproblems.

flashcards.front: Why are queues essential in print spooling systems?
flashcards.back: FIFO processing ensures documents print in arrival order, preventing resource conflicts.

flashcards.front: Which structure forms the basis for text editor undo/redo operations?
flashcards.back: Stack - LIFO behavior allows reversing recent operations first.

flashcards.front: How do linked lists enable efficient polynomial representation?
flashcards.back: Each node stores coefficient and exponent, allowing dynamic term management.

flashcards.front: What queue variation handles CPU scheduling in operating systems?
flashcards.back: Priority queue - processes with higher priority get executed first.

flashcards.front: Why are circular queues used in streaming data buffers?
flashcards.back: Efficient memory reuse through modulo operations on front/rear pointers.

flashcards.front: Which structure underlies BFS graph traversal implementation?
flashcards.back: Queue - ensures nodes are processed in discovery order.

flashcards.front: How do arrays accelerate matrix operations in scientific computing?
flashcards.back: Contiguous memory allows vectorized operations and cache optimization.

flashcards.front: What makes linked lists suitable for music playlists?
flashcards.back: Easy insertion/deletion between tracks without data shifting.

flashcards.front: Why are deques used in palindrome checking algorithms?
flashcards.back: Simultaneous access to both ends enables efficient character comparison.

flashcards.front: Which structure manages function calls in program execution?
flashcards.back: Call stack - tracks return addresses and local variables.
